sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=34, col=10).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 2, level 1.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Level 1 has a black background with several objects: - Two red cross markers (top-right at ~row10/col52, bottom-left at ~row52/col10) - A yellow vertical bar (rows 28-35, cols 9-11) with a red dot at row 34 - A green horizontal bar above the yellow bar (row 27, cols 9-11) - A top container (rows 18-24, cols 36-48) with two N-colored U-shapes in dark gray boxes - A bottom container (rows 35-47, cols 21-27) with two Y-colored U-shapes in dark gray boxes, separated by green bar - Another green bar at rows 9-11, col 27
- Goal model: Unknown yet - likely involves clicking objects to trigger transformations or movements. The yellow bar with red dot may be a cursor/agent. The containers hold colored shapes that may need to be matched or moved.
- Action model: Only MOUSE action available. Need to probe by clicking on different objects.
- Plan: Click on the yellow bar (near the red dot) to test if it's interactive, then observe what changes.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 1 of about 20 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The board changed after clicking the red dot. Let me compare the before and after states to see what changed. The key difference I notice is:
- Before: Node 29 was `G, pixels=64` at `rows 63-63, cols 0-63` (full bottom row)
- After: Node 29 is `G, pixels=63` at `rows 63-63, cols 0-62` and there's a new Node 30: `c, pixels=1` at `rows 63-63, cols 63-63`
So the bottom row (which seems to be a timer/progress bar) changed - one cell turned from G (dark gray) to c (charcoal). This is likely a HUD element (timer/step counter) that decrements with each action.
The gameplay objects themselves don't seem to have changed. Let me verify this more carefully by comparing the frames.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Compare before and after to see what actually changed
before = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation['nodes']
after = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']

print("=== Comparing before/after frames ===")
print(f"Before nodes: {len(before)}, After nodes: {len(after)}")

# Compare each node by color and approximate position
before_dict = {}
for n in before:
    key = (n['color'], n['pixels'], n['hash'])
    before_dict[key] = n

after_dict = {}
for n in after:
    key = (n['color'], n['pixels'], n['hash'])
    after_dict[key] = n

# Find differences
all_keys = set(list(before_dict.keys()) + list(after_dict.keys()))
for key in sorted(all_keys):
    if key in before_dict and key not in after_dict:
        print(f"  REMOVED: color={key[0]}, pixels={key[1]}, hash={key[2]}")
    elif key not in before_dict and key in after_dict:
        print(f"  ADDED: color={key[0]}, pixels={key[1]}, hash={key[2]}")
    else:
        # Check if position changed
        b = before_dict[key]
        a = after_dict[key]
        if b['boundary'] and a['boundary']:
            if b['boundary'] != a['boundary']:
                print(f"  MOVED: color={key[0]}, pixels={key[1]}")

print("\nBottom row check (timer bar):")
ascii_before = last_transition.before_frame.ascii.split('\n')
ascii_after = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print(f"  Before row 63: {ascii_before[63][:20]}...{ascii_before[63][-10:]}")
print(f"  After  row 63: {ascii_after[63][:20]}...{ascii_after[63][-10:]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
So the only change was in the bottom row (row 63) - one cell changed from G to c. This is a timer/progress bar that counts down with each action. The gameplay objects didn't change at all.
This means clicking the red dot on the yellow bar didn't do anything meaningful to the puzzle. I need to try clicking on other objects.
Let me think about what to click next. The main interactive-looking objects are:
1. The two containers (top one with N-shapes, bottom one with Y-shapes)
2. The red cross markers
3. The green bars
4. The yellow bar itself (not just the red dot)
Let me try clicking on one of the containers - maybe the top container with the N-shaped objects. Or maybe I should click on the red cross markers since they look like target indicators.
Actually, looking more carefully at the layout:
- The yellow bar with red dot is like a "player" or cursor
- The red crosses might be targets or waypoints
- The containers hold colored shapes
Maybe the game is about clicking the right container or shape. Let me try clicking on one of the N-shapes inside the top container, or one of the Y-shapes in the bottom container.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: The bottom row is a timer/progress bar (decrements with each action). Clicking the red dot on the yellow bar had no gameplay effect - only the timer changed. The actual puzzle objects remain unchanged.
Goal model: Still unknown. Need to find which objects are interactive. The containers with colored shapes seem most likely to be the puzzle elements.
Action model: MOUSE clicks on objects. The timer bar decrements regardless.
Plan: Try clicking on the N-shaped object inside the top container to test if containers are interactive.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Click on the left N-shape inside the top container (center around row 21, col 39)
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 21, 'col': 39}])
print(f"Action result: {last_action_result}")

# Check what changed - compare before and after
before = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation['nodes']
after = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']

# Build comparison
before_by_hash = {n['hash']: n for n in before}
after_by_hash = {n['hash']: n for n in after}

changed_hashes = set()
for h in set(list(before_by_hash.keys()) + list(after_by_hash.keys())):
    if h in before_by_hash and h not in after_by_hash:
        changed_hashes.add(('removed', h, before_by_hash[h]))
    elif h not in before_by_hash and h in after_by_hash:
        changed_hashes.add(('added', h, after_by_hash[h]))
    else:
        b = before_by_hash[h]
        a = after_by_hash[h]
        if b['boundary'] != a['boundary']:
            changed_hashes.add(('moved', h, a))

print(f"\nChanged objects: {len(changed_hashes)}")
for item in changed_hashes:
    if item[0] == 'moved':
        n = item[2]
        print(f"  MOVED: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, hash={n['hash']}")
    else:
        n = item[2]
        print(f"  {item[0].upper()}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, hash={n['hash']}")

# Also check if any gameplay objects changed (not the timer)
print("\nNon-timer objects summary:")
for n in after:
    if n['pixels'] > 2 and n['pixels'] < 200 and n['color'] != 'G' or (n['color'] in ['N', 'Y', 'c', 'g']):
        if n['boundary']:
            rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
            cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
            if min(rows) < 62:  # Not the timer row
                print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, bbox=({min(rows)},{min(cols)})-({max(rows)},{max(cols)})")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
